- People who've used
psychedelics are accused by their friends and
neighbors of "becoming nicer." Psychedelics shift your basic awareness of what perception even is. There are a number of ways in
which we shift our perception. Meditation, fasting, psychedelics is different than the others- and the difference in
the way it affects you is the difference between
walking up a mountain or being catapulted to
the top almost instantly. You have enhanced
capacity for understanding a level of complexity of life
that you didn't know you had. It's a realistic question to say, "What's the value of
having expanded vision, expanded hearing, expanded
awareness in general?" But it's the same question as,
"Why should we go to school? Why should we learn to read? Why should we visit other countries?" Because to transcend those
limits shifts forever the way in which you see the world. I'm James Fadiman. I've been involved in psychedelic research and experience for over 60 years. One of the great spiritual traditions has people asking, "Who
am I, who am I, who am I?" And the answer, whatever
your answer is, is limited. 'Transpersonal psychology' is a framework which allows you to get a larger view. It's simply a larger tool kit
than conventional psychology for looking at the central
thing that makes humans, humans which is consciousness. It includes all of awareness, all of consciousness,
all human experience. And it looks around
for what are the tools? Meditation is a tool. Psychedelics are a different kind of tool for opening up the
closed box of awareness. It's the natural evolution
of consciousness to expand. There comes a point for
many of us as adults where what we don't know feels dangerous and therefore we stop looking. Psychedelics break that pattern. They break open whatever
your system of belief is. Now, opening up consciousness means that everything is both
magnified and clarified. Think about it. Psychedelics go to every cell of the body, so when one reports that they
can smell color or hear color what they're saying is the
parts of the brain that normally are somewhat separated can
communicate with each other if we simply take down
the little guardrails. So that people report enhanced creativity because they can think of
more things simultaneously and farther away from
their original thought. What they also are aware of is their own relationships
with other people, particularly when they're
being used therapeutically, the negative patterns,
the things that prevent you being close to other people, become painfully visible because the central experience
that we all strive for is to be unified and connected to a larger reality than ourselves. Let's be clear, that like
any tool, it can be misused. Psychedelics not used
carefully, are dangerous. Cars not used carefully, are dangerous. Most things not used
carefully, can be dangerous. The problem for psychedelics is because the expansion is so strong, if you have no support, or no background, or no understanding, it's terrifying. After a psychedelic experience,
you are not only more open but you are questioning
very fundamental beliefs that you have had without
any particular knowledge. 'Integration' is a method
of dealing with the overflow of information that happens during and after a psychedelic experience. That time is a very fluid
time where the brain is still communicating more than usual and it's a good time
where someone can help you put together these very
unusual experiences so that you can retain the insights. We have a similar thing
if you think about it, if you're a refugee and you're coming from one country to another, people help you integrate
into the new culture. Integration in psychedelics
is simply a version of that more general kind of universal way of helping people move
from confusion to clarity. There's a tendency to
think of psychedelics as a personal experience,
but what we've found is that people who have used psychedelics are better members of their community. They have loosened up the
restrictions that keep us apart from each other because
one of the fundamentals that we all have is a sense of identity; kind of who lives in this box. With psychedelics, what you
realize is that in some sense you also are outside this box, so we are much more like trees than perhaps we want to imagine. Which is trees keep growing in every direction unless they're impeded but the natural is a full
expanding in each direction. That's perhaps the goal of
transpersonal psychology and it is the experience of psychedelics. The experience of psychedelics is that things will never
be the same afterwards because my Universe has
actually opened wider.

And as you tear the cap, there is a film that's clearly visible. And you can say, "Wow, that's a really big one." That film stretches then breaks. So these mushrooms have purple brown spores, a separable gelatinous pellicle, and they typically have a sharp umbo or a nipple at the very very top of the cap. And this papilla or nipple is not always in every specimen, but it's quite characteristic of the species in general. So psilocybe semilanceata. Many people when they pick semilanceata, they don't realize that the stem is so long. I think it's a mutualistic species with the rhizomes of grass. Um almost like a pseudo mycorrhizal relationship. Which means it's not obligatory, but it benefits from it. And the stems often are picked because they're people don't realize that the stem length is way way down. So be careful. This is such a special pleasure to have Paul 
Stamets rejoin us for the 2020 maiden voyage   of the Bioneers virtual conference. Paul 
first spoke at Bioneers in 1996 when he was   unknown. He showed up with this wild 
proposition that mushrooms can save   the world and he turned out to be right. It was 
clear from the outset that he's the rare genius   and visionary citizen scientist who can actually 
manifest his vision in the most practical terms.   Since that time, Paul has earned richly 
deserved major awards and worldwide recognition,   including the prestigious selection as 
a National Geographic explorer. He's the   central protagonist in the recent hit film that

written foundational books on mycelia--   I'm sorry -- and sparked a global mycelial 
awakening. His astonishing inventions in   mycotechnologies are addressing some of the 
world's most urgent global ecological and health   crises, from remediating toxic sites in farmlands 
to preventing bee colony collapse disorder,   to supporting our immune systems 
in these dangerous times. Today Paul will address the deepest 
underlying crisis of all the crises that   resides between our ears; it's a crisis 
of consciousness that's precipitating   the widespread awakening, that we are 
a part of nature not apart from it. So please join us in welcoming our beloved 
friend, colleague and mycelial ambassador,   Paul Stamets, live from British Columbia. Yeah, it’s been a long journey, Folks. I have been 
on this planet now for 65 years. My first interest   in mushrooms was finding psilocybin mushrooms, 
and indeed I wrote my first book when I was 23.   I just pulled it out and dusted it off. Psilocybi 
Mushrooms and Their Allies that contains—the   genus psilocybi contains the majority of the 
psilocybin mushrooms. There’s around 216 species   in the genus psilocybi. 116 species are psilocybin 
active, which means they contain the tryptamine   compounds psilocybin and psilocin, and some 
other interesting analogs that I’ll speak about. So, you know, it’s been an interesting course in 
my life, because I was originally—my interest in   this subject, when I went to conferences, I 
was treated like a leper; people avoided me.   They felt uncomfortable with the 
subject. My—Times have really changed. So I would like to first—I have a slide 
deck that’ll be available to all of you,   but I’m going to first really show you how much 
those times have changed. And what I want to do   is show three slides showing the universities that 
are currently engaged in research on psychedelics   and more specifically on psilocybin mushrooms. So 
if we have that first slide up, that’d be great. These are the institutions currently doing 
research on psychedelics. The next slide shows   the institutions specifically doing research on 
psilocybin mushrooms. Now this is extraordinary   to think that these institutions were approved 
by the FDA and the DEA – FDA’s over the DEA – for   clinical trials because they satisfied several 
important criteria. 1) They’re unlikely to do any   harm; 2) being able to address a critical medical 
need that’s not currently being addressed by   conventional medicine; and 3) is it scalable, can 
it be brought in as a medicine at an affordable   price. So, indeed, these benchmarks have been 
met. There’s now up to 40 institutions that are   currently doing clinical studies on psilocybin, 
and even in Europe I think there’s around – next   slide if we could – we have about 20 institutions 
in Europe also. So this is a global movement. What’s astonishing about this is the course 
of conclusions all steer towards the same   endpoints – that these are some of the most 
powerful psychoactive and psycho—psychiatric   medicines ever been discovered by medicine. 
But Indigenous Peoples – and we all are   indigenous to this planet – but Indigenous Peoples 
in Greece, in Spain, in North Africa, in South   America and Central America, Mesoamerica, North 
America have long ago discovered that these were   sacred medicines to be treated with respect, 
and they can help the community psychologically. So looking at the toxicity scales of different 
drugs, psilocybin is unique in that it is   one of the most extraordinarily nontoxic 
medicines that the FDA has ever considered.   So we are now seeing a revolution across the 
planet, literally, as Nina and Kenny like to   say, a revolution from the underground. 
Well, that’s exactly what is happening. So many of you know that Oakland was the first to 
decriminalize psychoactive plants and psilocybin   in particular. It was followed by Denver and most 
recently Washington, D.C. in the belly of the   beast. I think more than 70 percent of the voters 
voted for the decriminalization of psychedelics.   And then most recently, of course, in 
Oregon – ballot measures 109 and 110,   on the therapeutic use of 
psilocybin to be legalized,   and the decriminalization of drugs, ballot 
110. So this is the will of the people. So politicians, government officials, take note.   This is the will of the people. The 
people are voting to decriminalize these   sacred substances so they can be responsibly 
used, you know, for helping people in need. What is so extraordinary about these 
medicines is that their widespread effects   address many issues currently that are not 
being addressed by conventional medicines,   especially with PTSD, with trauma associated 
with other challenges that we face in life,   with addiction, with opioids, with tobacco. A 
more recent study from Johns Hopkins showing   that a heroic dose of psilocybin combined with 
therapy shows up to a greater than 60 percent   reduction not only in the tobacco use but in 
the two-year window subsequently people were   able to break their tobacco addiction, one of the 
most addictive substances that we’re aware of. And so I want to just, you know, emphasize the 
importance that the tides are changing. We have   come to a point now to recognize that psilocybin 
and psilocybin mushrooms are appropriate medicines   for our time. We all suffer from some 
form of trauma right now because of COVID.   We are stressed, many of us, to our limits. 
We’ve never had challenges like this before.   Many, many Indigenous Peoples have 
suffered from much greater challenges   than that which most of us are suffering 
from now, so this is unfortunately—The   road has been unfortunately paved by the trauma 
of many people before we’ve seen COVID. But we are   all on this Earthship together, so it’s important 
that we act in unity and with compassion. One of the things that’s so interesting 
about psilocybin mushrooms is that with   the experience of psilocybin mushrooms, 
there’s an enormous reduction in depression   and anxiety. We have actually launched an app 
at Microdose.me. It’s for the Apple platform.   It’s been—It’s anonymized. It has gone through 
a board review to make sure that it conforms   to health professional standards. And we have 
had over 14,000 subscribers to Microdose.me. It   measures mood, anxiety, depression, agility. 
There’s a tap test that’s important for   determining if someone has pre-Parkinson-like 
symptoms, the ability of your being able to tap   your fingers as quickly as possible in 60 
seconds. It’s got hearing, it’s got sight,   it’s got memory. And we have now 
just uncovered some of the data sets,   and the data sets from microdosing with psilocybin 
mushrooms are revealing profound results. Although many of these institutions, 
in fact all of them that I know of,   have been using pure psilocybin or psilocin, 
psilocybin dephosphorylates in the psilocin,   so when you take psilocybin either 
in pure substance or in a mushroom,   it becomes psilocin and that crosses into the 
blood/brain barrier. It’s a 5H2A antagonist,   which means it substitutes for serotonin, 
your most predominant neurotransmitter. So in looking at the clinical studies using 
pure psilocybin or psilocin is it clearly   shows tremendously positive effects in offsetting 
depression with long-term positive results. What’s   interesting about some of the Johns Hopkins 
studies with PTSD is that with the psilocybin   experience, an intensive one, with therapy with 
follow-up therapeutic consultations, many of these   patients have resolved their PTSD trauma. And 
what’s so interesting about it is re-remembering   the curative session with psilocybin appears to 
supplant the PTSD experience. And so the mere act   of re-remembering it has a therapeutic benefit. 
Now this is really interesting because it suggests   that there are a different chain of neurons, 
of neurological sequences that become resonant,   and this becomes the predominant reflection 
upon your life; you’ll remember how wonderful   it was to have that resolution of your trauma 
through a high-dose, guided psilocybin trip. Now this also brings into focus some 
other issues that I want to bring up,   is that are psilocybin mushrooms less equal or 
more beneficial than psilocybin in pure form?   There is a rush right now to commercialize 
psilocybin. At least 20 companies have been   created for the commercialization of psilocybin 
into a pharmaceutical drug. And though I do   applaud everyone’s effort to use psilocybin to 
resolve many of the issues I just described,   I’m also very, very concerned about the 
commercialization of a sacred substance. With the Microdose.me app, it was not with pure 
psilocybin. It was psilocybin mushrooms. And our   results thus far, using the DAS scale, which is 
a depression/anxiety index, the standard practice   by psychiatrists, and the PANAS scale, which is 
another scale that measures mood, positive mood   and negative mood, with both these scales using 
microdosing – and microdosing is defined as taking   a sub-sensorium dose of psilocybin; that means 
below a threshold that you would experience it.   So if you take some psilocybin mushrooms 
and you feel them, that’s not a microdose.   So a microdose with psilocybi cubensis is around 
1/10th of a gram. I have lift off in myself – I’m   pretty experienced in this – but I have lift 
off from around a third to a half a gram. There is normalization over time. So if you 
take a dose of psilocybin, even if you have   a lift-off dose later on, you normalize. But 
the sub-sensorium microdose translates into   about 1/10th of a gram of psilocybi cubensis, 
the most commonly available psilocybin mushroom   currently, and with those microdoses which 
is done periodically through the week. And with our Microdose.me app, there’s three 
confounders to our data that I want to underscore.   Number 1, you have variability in the potency 
of the psilocybin mushrooms you're obtaining.   Are they a year old? How were they grown? 
How were they stored? You know, etc.,   etc. Then you also have the variability and 
the receptivity of the individuals that are   talking the psilocybin mushrooms. Some people 
are non-responders. This is hugely important.   And then the third, which I sort of alluded 
to, was the frequency of how often you're   talking them. Are you talking them once a 
week, three times a week, five times a week? Despite those three confounders, when 
we unveiled the data, we found that the   P-values of significance. And many of you know 
what this is, but for those that you don't,   the P-value is a statistical measurement of 
predicted significance. If the P-value is .05,   you have 95 percent confidence. Our P-values 
were .00001. Unambiguously significant. So the fact that psilocybin mushrooms 
are giving such positive results   that are competitive or comparative, depending 
on your point of view, the pure psilocybin   being promoted by these new pharmaceutical 
startup companies, I think, is an example of   people medicine versus profit medicine. Now 
I would like to take both sides of the fence   here in this argument. What is people medicine? 
Well, people medicine is psilocybin mushrooms are   available for the people, people can grow them at 
home, you can grow them in your backyard; there’s   lots of opportunities for acquiring them without 
having a physician permission or prescription. So the problem with psilocybin mushrooms 
that I mentioned is the variability.   The problem also is the fact that 
you don't know the chain of custody   where it came from. If you have an 
adverse reaction, who do you talk to? And then the standardization, not being 
certain, means that the variability can   be substantial from one experience to another. 
So there’s obviously quality control problems. And another one that’s a big issue with 
me is taxes. The underground psilocybin   mushroom economy is not paying their 
fair share of taxes. They use the roads,   they use the fire department, the police 
department, all the social benefits from the   Post Office, etc., and they are basically 
doing commercial transactions tax free.   Okay. I think that can be solved through 
the decriminalization and the licensing of   quality controlled psilocybin mushroom suppliers. 
That I think will be happening in Oregon first. So—But the other side of the coin, and in 
defense or in favor, of the pharmaceutical-grade   psilocybin is the fact that you can get a 
standardized amount, you do know the chain   of custody where it came from, you do know 
where to go if you have an adverse reaction,   and of course it is taxable. There is a 
tax revenue going back for the sale of   these substances. But nevertheless, I think the 
benefits of psilocybin mushrooms far outweigh   the benefits of psilocybin. And that’s 
specifically because of the entourage effect. For the past several years, we’ve been 
looking at the psilocybin analogues.   Psilocybin mushrooms contain several other 
tryptamines that are metabolically in the chain   of production of psilocybin or its degradation 
– baeocystin, norbaeocystin, and norpsilocin.   We have found that baeocystin, 
norbaeocystin, and norpsilocin,   which are not psychoactive – they won't get you 
high – but they produce a strong neurogenesis.   And in the slides that you can see, we do 
experiments compared to controls, saline controls,   brain-derived neurotrophic factors, BDNFs, and 
then when we spike these neurons from pluripotent   stem cells grown in vitro, with aliquots of 
baeocystin, norbaeocystin and norpsilocin, all   those compounds are legal. And we found something 
extraordinary. Not only did it stimulate neurons   to grow within seven to 12 days, but when we 
started stacking them, and especially with   Lion’s Mane, we had greater than the arithmetic 
predicted effect. And what that means is that when   some of these neurons increased by eight 
percent or 12 percent within their sample,   the additive effect would be 20 percent, you would 
think. No, we had about a 36 percent increase. So the entourage effect of these 
naturally occurring psilocybin analogues,   and we have found stacking it with 
Lion’s Mane mushroom mycelium-based   products then creates an entourage effect 
of neurogenesis. This is so important   because I think psilocybin is an Einstein 
molecule. Psilocybin makes you smarter.   Psilocybin makes you kinder. 
Psilocybin makes you more courageous. And in the slide decks that you'll see, there 
are several meta studies that are extraordinary.   A single dose of psilocybin is closely correlated 
with a reduction to partner-to-partner violence;   reducing larceny by more than 20 
percent; violent crime by 18 percent.   Now association’s not causation, but it can 
be. And the more these studies progress,   it looks like psilocybin mushrooms do 
make for better citizens, better people. When someone is traumatized, it not only 
traumatizes themselves and the victim,   but it traumatizes the families of both, and 
not only their families but their neighbors and   their communities and their cities and their 
countries. So this is a worldwide uprising.   I think when people realize that 
psilocybin mushrooms can resolve trauma. And so I hope all of you come to understand 
that these need to be used responsibly.   It’s very important that we don't lose this 
opportunity. In the 1960s, it was lost.   We have to treat these substances as sacred 
medicines. We have a panel later on today.   We’ll talk about the indigenous use of these 
sacraments, and how does psilocybin mushrooms   fit into that context with an indigenous 
history of the use of these sacred substances. So with that, because time is so short, I want to 
say thank you very much, Kenny and Nina. I’m just   speaking into a box here. So thank you so much 
for everything you've done for the community.   Thank you for giving me this voice, and I hope all 
of us survive this shared crisis together, and we   come out as a better people, a kinder people, more 
courageous and with greater wisdom. Thank you.

The mushrooms they ate with honey when they began to get heated from them, they began to dance and some sang and some wept. Some cared not to sin, but would sit down in their rooms and stay there pensive-like. Then these when the drunkenness of the mushrooms had passed, they spoke one with another about the visions that they had seen. So, this is recorded or re-recorded in a excellent monograph by Roger Heim and R. Gordon Wasson in Les Champignons Hallucinogènes du Mexique which was a basically a treatise on many of the new species that were unknown to science, but known to indigenous people and they were recorded. Unfortunately, when Les Champignons Hallucinogènes du Mexique was published, it was published a few weeks after Rolf Singer and Alexander Smith published a monograph in their journal called Mycologia. And because they published these same species first and gave them names, then unfortunately, much of the work and the names that is were usurped by Singer and Smith. And so the famous Psilocybe Wassonii then became secondary species epithet and other species then prevailed in the scientific literature. This is the way it goes in science. Whoever publishes the names first in the scientific literature and deposited specimens in herbaria, their names prevail. So if it was the the graciousness of Maria Sabina a Wahacan Mazatec shaman who introduced psilocybe mushrooms to R. Gordon Wasson and Valentina Wasson. Now this is important for people to understand that Valentina Wasson was the mycologist. She was a Russian physician. She studied and knew mushrooms in Russia by Latin names. She knew many, many species and she was very much attracted to mushrooms when R. Gordon Wasson on their honeymoon in the Catskills when they found mushrooms, he was aghast at her enthusiasm and excitement for finding these beautiful mushrooms. He was he came from an English perspective of mycophobia and she was mycophilic. And so actually they invented those two terms, mycophilia and mycophobia because they were astonished at each other's cultural bias and affection in reacting to the presence of mushrooms. That led them on to a lifelong study of the ethnomycology of mushrooms. Now it's important because I met R. Gordon Wasson several times. I attended three lectures of him and in every single lecture he gave credit to Tina Wasson. He gave credit to her voluminously and often cried in tear in tears trying to express his gratitude to his wife who unfortunately died in 1957 from cancer. So at that time in 1957, Life magazine came out. It was distributed to millions of of Americans primarily on the doorsteps in the middle of the Cold War. And inside Life magazine was basically a field guide to the psilocybe mushrooms of Mexico. So, this is Psilocybe cubensis and this is a simplified life cycle just to bring everybody up to the same to the same level here. Mushrooms produce spores. The spores typically are sexual in that the two spores have to come together. They mate and then the downstream myceliums it was binucleate. One nucleus in each spore. When they go when they merge and they mate and form mycelium, then the mycelium has two nuclei per cell. And then, in this this is fertile mycelium is then incapable of reproducing a fruit body, a mushroom literally in a just a few days. So, the mycelium can be resident for weeks, months, even years and then under the influence of a drop in temperature, introduction of water, the mycelium wicks to the surface, exhales carbon dioxide, inhales oxygen, and then surprisingly light. Those four stimuli stimulate the mushroom mycelium to produce a reproductive structure called a fruit body, otherwise known as a mushroom. Now, I just love this photograph. This is one that's recent. Um, this is Psilocybe cyanescens in a garden. It's called a wavy cap. Now, if you look closely, you'll see a bluing reaction on the stem. And then these when you take a spore print, this is Psilocybe cyanofruticulosa, and you see the spores fall according to the radiating symmetry of the gills. But in the field of mycology, spore color is really important. So, I came up with a simple rule and it's a generality that if a mushroom has a purple-brown to black spore print and it bruises bluish in color, then it's almost certainly a psilocybin mushroom. Now, with every rule, there are exceptions. For those of you who want to know the exceptions, there's Psilocybe caerulipes, subcaerulipes, albocyanescens. These are other species that are that are bluish in color, have a bluish purple-brown spore print, but they are not psychoactive. Okay, but they're not deadly and they're not poisonous. But generally speaking, this rule stands true today. If a mushroom bruises bluish, a gilled mushroom, not a pored mushroom, a gilled mushroom, mushrooms have gills, and the spore print is purplish-brown to black, it's almost 95% certain it's a psilocybin mushroom. Of course, you must be able to identify mushrooms accurately in the wild and so, please be careful, consult an expert, and make sure you know what you're doing. Now, it's important that you know that Psilocybe cubensis is a very rapidly growing mushroom. So, these are ideal stages for consumption. The veils are closed. They're The veil extends from the cap edge to the stem. Um and um it protects the gills. But, what you're seeing these beautiful specimens right here with closed veils, in the matter of 2 to 6 hours, the caps expand. And even though the caps may look larger, the mushroom may look larger, there's no increase in mass. In fact, the flesh of the mushroom thins, and the gills get broader, and the gills have spores on them. There's no psilocybin in the spores. So, actually, the potency of the mushroom is declines, and the mass of the mushroom does not increase. So, this is a big concern because some people are very allergic to spores. And in fact, in a study of asthmatic children, 50 children, um they over 100% of them were allergic to Psilocybe cubensis spores. An extraordinary high number of asthmatic children. Now, asthmatic children become asthmatic adults. A good friend of mine, unfortunately, his wife died from an asthma attack. Um so, people should be concerned about harm reduction and safety. And this is a big concern I have, because after these mushrooms get large like this, and they have the appearance of being more, which might incentivize growers to produce larger mushrooms, their immune system of the mushroom declines radically. And so, they're not as strong in their ability to prevent contaminants, bacteria, other molds from growing. So, co-occurring with the maturation of these mushrooms is unfortunately contamination. So I came up with this formula in around 2015, 2014. And it's a combination of psilocybin, lion's mane mushrooms, and niacin, nicotinic acid, the flushing form. And I've been working a lot with lion's mane mushrooms for many, many decades. And there's lots of great research on it as a regeneration of myelin on the axons of nerves. And to be able to potentially be used for treatment of Alzheimer's, Parkinson's. And Dr. Kawagishi gets credit for this. 1994, he was looking for antibacterial agents. He stumbled upon lion's mane mushrooms having coding for nerve growth factors that cause neurons to regrow. And so I got really excited about this and I because of my experience with psilocybin and gourmet medicinal mushroom, it became a Venn intersection of interests. And then I thought about niacin. Well, it's well reported in the '60s and '70s, if you want to come down for a bad trip with psilocybin, you'll take niacin. And so there was it was common knowledge. Even my brother John told me about this. He had the federales in Colombia coming up the the the mountainside, and they all took a whole bunch of niacin, and they immediately came down. Of course, I think they would probably come down anyhow. But in any event, so I I looked at this and you know, I think they didn't quite get it right. Actually, I think niacin potentiates psilocybin in a microdose. And the idea being of the vasodilator, there'd be more blood flow. When you take niacin, if you haven't taken 50 mg 100 mg of niacin, try it. You'll in you know, about 15 minutes start itching, get red. And I thought by stimulating the endpoints of the peripheral nervous system, often times neuropathies show up as a deadening of the fingertips and the toes before the vascular system collapses. If you can get a vasodilator effect from niacin, then you can deliver these neurogenic compounds, psilocybin and in this case erinacines from lion's mane mushrooms, to the endpoint of the peripheral nervous system. Moreover, because of the itching flushing effect, if you were to take not 100 mg of niacin, but a gram by trying to take 10 times more psilocybin so you could get high, it'd be an aversive. It'd be like the Antabuse with alcoholics. I thought, well, I do a lot of in vitro propagation. I grow a lot of mycelium in culture. Cells take time to divide. And I thought to myself, by having the stimulus of one to four four days on, and then two to three days off, so it doesn't normalize, so you resensitize. But that will build the neurogenic pathway, allowing these nerves to be able to regrow. So that was my also my hypothesis. So this is really interesting because now there was some criticism about, "Well, it's not placebo." Well, this is an observational study. How do you do a placebo with an observational study? Placebos are done in controlled studies where there's a cause and effect with a drug and then a placebo. This is asking people to self-report. Now, we do agree that there could be an a bias, a response bias, because we found in the survey that people who are more depressed were more motivated to microdose. Interestingly, we had over 4,000 people who were non-microdosers with over 4,000 people who were microdosers. This was really surprising to us that so many non-microdosers would go to microdose.me to then also record their activity. I was wondering, did they want to get a baseline before they started microdosing? So we had a very nice balanced data set. And we found quite interestingly some major observational trends. Again, these are self-reported. We have a second paper that we have submitted. And the question about placebo is is very um well discussed, you might say, as well as expectancy bias. But in this study, we found with mood and mental health on the PANAS and DASS schedules, with the stack and with psilocybin in any form, the graphs are very, very similar. There was a massive reduction in the report of depression and anxiety. So hugely significant that people who microdose with psilocybin in any form had a reduction in depression and anxiety. And an improvement in positive mood and a reciprocal, but not as significant reduction in negative mood or the amplitude of which. So this was okay. This is we still have this problem of disambiguating really what's going on here. Is it people have a proclivity to microdose? Again, we get into the placebo effect, but without a controlled study, you don't know. But we thought, well, what in our microdose.me study is there something that would be independent of this placebo argument? And we found something truly extraordinary. And it speaks to the tap test. This is a alternate tap test, the the validated test for Parkinson's, Alzheimer's, traumatic brain injury, um dementia. And in Parkinson's as an example, there is no increase in the tap test. Now, what is the tap test? The tap test is alternating fingers, and it can be finger to to thumb, but in this case because of the iPhone and the app that was been developed, we did it specifically for um for the iPhone, so you can have a double tap alternating back and forth. How many times can you tap in 10 seconds? So, we had everybody, including the non-microdosers, uh who participated, uh thousands of people um engage in the tap test. And so, all this data was blind to us. There we have about eight co-authors on this. The statisticians, the biostatisticians got all the data. None of us co-authors ever saw it. We had no ability of of even looking at it. And then we found something truly extraordinary. Well, at first, we looked at between the under the age of 55 and over the age of 55. interdisciplinary topic in the realm of drugs than psychedelics. They've apparently been
used for time immemorial throughout many different

after Albert Hofmann had first discovered the

Osmond in Saskatchewan, Canada. They were really the first group that I would say developed what we now call "psychedelic therapy." There was this growing
appreciation of some of the risks and the safeguards needed
to minimize those risks, but not every investigator
was aware of this. There was some unethical
research, some wild research. There was controversy
surrounding Timothy Leary, especially after he
was fired from Harvard. - 'Turn on, tune in, and drop out.' - Psychedelics became so

that were running sessions and got the notices they just had to stop. The rug is pulled out for decades before there's a reinitiation. The research that's happening now, a lot of it is exploring
medical applications. These are all drugs that
can have a profound effect on one's sense of reality,
including one's sense of self. I think kind of the
only bet you can make is that if you have the dose high enough, something really
interesting is gonna happen. You might find yourself
in your 'mind's eye,' soaring in the heavens; the secrets of the Universe
seemingly revealed to you. On the other hand, five seconds later it might collapse into what
folks call a 'bad trip,' where one could feel
that they're gonna die. It's sort of this like massive shake up in one's experience of reality. I'm floored when people with really substantial life experience will say things like, 'This was the most meaningful
experience of their life.' Compared to the intensity
of the subjective effects, it's remarkable how little impact these substances have on physiology. You know, your heart
rate, your blood pressure- everything has its risk, to be clear. But compared to other drug classes, psychedelics are really robustly safe at the physiological level. My therapeutic research with
psychedelics is primarily used 'psilocybin,' which is the
agent in magic mushrooms. The dose we now give
to patients is anywhere from 30 milligrams to 40 milligrams, which Terence McKenna, who's
the famous psychedelic bard would refer to repeatedly
as the "heroic dose." If a microdose is over here and a recreational dose is
over here, we're way over here. When someone comes in for the day of their psychedelic
session, they've already spent several hours with us
on previous days preparing for the experience,
developing that rapport. We know that all drugs are
in part shaped by the context in which they're given, but
for the psychedelic substances, that's particularly more so. So when you provide it in more of a warm, welcoming situation, you tend to get more meaningful
reactions to the substance. When the experience unfolds the person is gonna lay
down, wear eye shades, and headphones through
which music is played. So the idea is: kinda get out of your normal, everyday,
discursive analyzing intellect. We really just encourage them
to trust, let go, and be open. Trust the overall process. Going into this type of
treatment is not easy at all. Trust yourself that you're robust: You're gonna get through this experience. Let go. What does let go mean? Let go means whatever
your preconceived notions of what this experience might be or what experience you should have, don't let that get in the
way for the experience that you will have. Don't try to control it, and be open no matter where this takes you, go there. Whatever the experience is,
take the orientation of: 'This is what I need to deal with.' The range of experiences
are just so broad. You can swing from a terrifying experience to this beautific, you know, sort of like, most amazing
experience of your life. Some people could feel like
they've reached the end of reality, this kind
of plane of existence. Others say that they
can feel the suffering across the entire planet. You know, sort of having
this sort of experience of 'universal empathy.' It's all welcome. The laughter, the tears,
everything in between. That's exactly what this process is for. So the 'integration phase'
is where we're discussing the experience with them, and the themes that come up are so varied. One of the aspects you
could call 'revealed truth,' and I call these 'duh moments.' These are experiences
where people will feel like they've learned something
at such a fundamental level. I think of examples in our work we've done with cancer patients where, you know, oftentimes people will say, "I'm allowing this to
create my own suffering. The cancer hasn't killed me yet. I can still live life, but
I'm letting it do this to me." They knew it up here, they
could tell you the words, but now they feel it in their bones- they feel it in their heart. People are reminded about this power they have to choose their own destiny. Some people come out of these
sessions as participants and say that, you know, "One 6-hour experience with psilocybin is like a
thousand hours of therapy." But one of the pitfalls
of this clinical work is that you'd be hard pressed
to find a more intimate and vulnerable psychological
intervention for a person. That's just a situation
that is ripe for abuses if we don't have the right safeguards. Because of that vulnerability, because of the incredible
gravity of these sessions, we need to even more tenaciously cling to clinical boundaries. We need to limit the use
of therapeutic touch. A subtler form of abuse is just filling in the blanks for people- I call it the guru complex. People often confront
big picture questions: What is the meaning of life? Does God exist? What's the big picture of
everything that's going on? What am I? On those metaphysical questions, we really have to be silent. In the biggest picture, aside
from psychedelic's ability to treat one disorder or another, I think they're just
poised to really help us to understand the nature
of psychiatric disorders. What commonalities do
they have with each other that are somehow being addressed
by psychedelic treatment? I kind of think of all of these disorders as different forms of addiction. So whether it's to a substance
like tobacco or alcohol, cocaine, or a way of
thinking about yourself, it's this narrowed mental
and behavioral repertoire: reality used to be like this, and now your reality is like this. And psychedelics, when done
well in the right setting, have the ability to just blow people out of that narrowed story. It holds a lot of promise
in helping us figure out what it takes for the human
being to be mentally healthy. - Get smarter, faster, with videos from the world's biggest thinkers. To learn even more from the

Um and so we went to Crater Lake Lodge in Oregon and we're at the lodge and we're having lunch and this bus boy comes up and he's about 16 years of age or 17 and he goes, "Don't interrupt a stutterer. Just smile. Be at peace with it." you know, and he finally goes, "Can I help you?" I went, "Wow, that's exactly the way I stuttered." And so I told him, "I stuttered exactly like you." === VIDEO: fVlDA5GGT_Q (https://youtu.be/fVlDA5GGT_Q) ===

Imagine if the leap from ape to human didn't start with tools or fire, but with a trip, and not a metaphorical one, either. According to one of the strangest and most controversial ideas in anthropology, early humans evolved partly because they ate psychedelic mushrooms. It's called the stoned ape theory, and it asks a wild question. What if psilocybin was the secret ingredient behind human consciousness? It's a kind of idea that sounds like a plot to a science fiction novel, but it's a real hypothesis, and it's captured the imagination of psychonauts, mystics, and even curious scientists ever since. But before we dismiss it or believe it, let's see where the evidence actually leads. In the book, Food of the Gods, Terrence McKenna laid out one of the most controversial and psychedelic ideas ever proposed about human evolution. that the leap from homo erectus to homo sapiens was triggered not by fire tools or language but by psilocybin mushrooms. He believed this shift happened during a critical environmental turning point. Around 2 million years ago much of Africa was drying out. The lush tropical forest that once supported early hominins began to shrink. Climate shifts led to increasing desertification, pushing our ancestors out of the trees and into open savas. Fruit became harder to find and the old forest based diets were no longer reliable. So our ancestors, like many species under pressure, adapted. They began following herds of grazing animals across the grasslands, scavenging whatever they could. From insects to tubers to whatever they found growing in the dung left behind by large herbivores. And this is where McKenna's theory kicks in. He proposed that while foraging in this new environment, our ancestors came across psilocybus, a psychoactive mushroom that naturally grows in cattle tongue. Curious and hungry, they ate it. And according to McKenna, that moment repeated over generations sparked nothing less than a cognitive revolution. He claimed that at low doses, the psilocybin sharpened visual acuity, specifically edge detection, making early humans better hunters. They could spot predators, track prey, and navigate their surroundings more effectively. McKenna cited research from the 1960s by Roland Fischer and others who showed that psychedelics can heighten certain types of sensory perception. At slightly higher doses, he believed that the mushroom increased sexual arousal and energy, giving individuals a reproductive edge. Those who consumed the mushroom were more alert, more active, and more likely to mate, passing on their genes and giving rise to a population with both behavioral and neurological advantages. But the most radical part of McKenna's theory comes with the higher doses. He believed that psilocybin in these larger amounts helped dissolve the ego and blur the boundaries between self and other. This, he argued, would have promoted strong social bonding, communal rituals, and even group sexual practices. McKenna imagined early tribes gathering together in mushroom induced ecstasy, forming deep connections that led to greater genetic mixing and a shared sense of responsibility for the group's offspring. He also believed that these experiences were deeply spiritual. The visions, the sense of awe, the disintegration of the self, all of it, all of it, he thought, gave rise to the very first religious impulses. Our ancestors weren't just high. They were having what felt like encounters with the divine. Most importantly, McKenna argued that these altered states of consciousness stimulated activity in the brain regions tied to language, imagination, and symbolic thought. He claimed that this was the real origin of art, music, and spoken language. The mushroom wasn't just a drug. It was a neurological key that unlocked higher cognition. To McKenna, psilocybin mushrooms weren't just part of the diet. They were the driving force behind everything we now associate with being human. culture, religion, storytelling, scientific thinking. It all began in his eyes with the discovery of a psychedelic fungus growing an animal tongue on the African savannah. It's a theory that sounds a little absurd on the surface, but it touches on something primal, something about curiosity, transformation, and the way altered states might shape who we are. McKenna's theory was not accepted by mainstream paleo anthropology, but his writing continued to live in the public consciousness. In recent years, the theory has gained some popularity with micologist Paul Stammits. Micologist study fungi, and though Stammits is mostly self-taught, he's widely recognized for advancing mushroom cultivation and public awareness of micology. At a 2017 psychedelic science conference, Stammit stepped up to defend and modernize Terrence McKenna's stoned ape theory. In his talk, Psilocybin mushrooms and the micology of consciousness, Stammits argued that the sudden doubling of the human brain starting 2 million years ago remains a mystery and that psychedelics might offer a plausible explanation. Echoing McKenna, he imagined that early humans wandering the savannah, stumbling across psilocybin mushrooms growing from dung, eating them, and then experiencing dramatic shifts in perception and cognition. Stammits didn't claim to prove the idea, but he urged listeners to consider it a very, very plausible hypothesis for the rapid evolution of homo sapiens. The stone ape theory is undeniably fascinating. It's bold, imaginative, and taps into some of the deepest questions about consciousness and our early evolution. But now, it's time to take a closer look and see if his ideas stand up to scrutiny. First, a quick clarification. Despite the name, it's not actually a theory in the scientific sense. It's a hypothesis, an intriguing idea based on limited evidence, one that hasn't been rigorously tested or widely accepted by the scientific community. In everyday language, we often use theory to mean a guess. But in science, a theory is something far more substantial, a wellsupported explanation backed by a strong body of evidence. If you take one thing away from this video, it is that the word theory and a hypothesis are not interchangeable. Okay, now let's dive into early evolution, one of my favorite topics. McKenna posited that around 2 million years ago, much of Africa was drying out. The lush tropical forest that once supported early hominins began to shrink. Climate shifts led to increasing desertification, pushing our ancestors out of the trees and into open savas. He is partially correct in this assessment as Africa was drying out and the savannah was expanding between 2.8 to 2.5 million years ago. However, his idea about our ancestors being pushed under the savannah is largely out of date. We know that hominins had been living in open environments long before the genus Homo even [Music] appeared. The oustralopythesines, the predecessors to our genus, appeared over 4 million years ago. They were adapted to open environments as well as sparsely wooded environments. We know for a fact that the earliest species in our genus, such as Homoerectus and Homohabilis, were perfectly adapted to open environments as far as 2 million years ago. So their ridification of Africa didn't force early humans onto the savannah, but rather they were already adapted to it. But this might actually play in McKenna's favor as in these open environments, dung loving psychedelic mushrooms were common. Genetic analysis suggests that psilocybis, one of the most well-known magic mushrooms, likely existed across Africa well over 2 million years ago. However, they may have only been available during wetter seasons of the year. McKenna hypothesized that early hominids looking in the dung of large herbivores for grubs would have often come across these mushrooms. This is possible as other primate species today such as baboons and chimpanzees flip over dong and dig through it to find beetle larvae and grubs. Assuming that early humans would have tried these mushrooms from time to time, it is plausible that they recognize them as an entertaining and stimulating experience. After all, a handful of animals appear to seek out recreational substances. For example, multiple species, including chimps, will eat fermented fruit, seemingly to enjoy the alcohol. Some monkeys in the Caribbean have become full-on alcoholics, and dolphins have been observed inhaling puffer fish toxins and acting inebriated. It is entirely possible that early hominins recognized the effects of mushrooms and enjoyed them. But could these substances have given them an edge? At the heart of the stone egg hypothesis is the idea that consuming psilocybin mushrooms gave early hominins a survival advantage. perhaps enhancing cognition, perception, or social bonding in ways that accelerated brain evolution. But from an evolutionary and scientific standpoint, this claim is highly speculative and lacks empirical support. To begin with, let's address McKenna's claims that low doses of psilocybin mushrooms can enhance visual acuity, particularly edge detection, giving early humans an advantage in hunting and survival. This is one of McKenna's most specific and most misunderstood claims. According to him, sharper eyesight meant better tracking of prey, quicker spotting of predators, and ultimately more food and offspring. He often cited experiments by pharmacologist Roland Fisher to support this idea, claiming that his work showed that psilocybin improved vision in measurable ways. But here's the problem. That study didn't actually test visual acuity. What Fischer's team measured was depth perception and spatial distortion, not the ability to see fine details or sharpened visual edges. McKenna essentially took a study about altered perception and spun it into a story about enhanced vision. Furthermore, Fischer Edall stated that psilocybin may not be conductive to the survival of an organism. To this day, no peer-reviewed research has shown that psilocybin improves human visual acuity. It alters perception. Yes, it intensifies colors, distorts space, and can even make the world feel hyperdetailed, but those effects are subjective and not the same as objectively seeing better. In fact, most controlled studies show that psychedelics introduce visual distortions, not clarity. Even modern micro doing trials, where people take tiny amounts of psychedelics to boost performance, have failed to show consistent benefits to perception or cognition. So, did psilocybin make our ancestors see like hawks? Probably not. McKenna's claim is imaginative, but it stretches the data beyond what the science supports. The idea of sharper vision under mushrooms may feel true to someone in the moment, but from an evolutionary standpoint, there's just no proof that this would actually work. Another one of McKenna's claims was that the use of these mushrooms led to a rapid increase in the size of the human brain. This is a legitimately interesting question. Why did the human brain supposedly double in a period of roughly 2 million years? Well, let's break down the increase in the human brain size based on species. Our genus evolved from oral epipthesines. Smallbodied upright walking homminids with a brain size of around 400 to 550 cm. Then with the emergence of our genus was homohabilis dating at 2.4 million years ago with a brain size of 500 to 775 cm. Then Homoerectus appeared two million years ago. First with a slightly larger brain and then by around 500,000 years ago, their descendants commonly had brains over a thousand cubic centimeters. By the time our species appeared 300,000 years ago, hominids all over the world had brains larger than ours today, more than 1500 cm. This includes Neandertols, our ancestors, and East Asian hominids like Homolongi and likely Denisven. Point being, the human brain size continued to increase around the world in varied environments. It wasn't unique to our species or to the savannah. Hominins in Europe to Southeast Asia were involving increasingly larger brains except homo feres ances but they were getting really small. It is difficult to reconcile this with the stoned ape theory. The idea that mushroom use from animal dung on the savannah led to our larger brains and higher intelligence doesn't square with our evidence. What about the other populations that may have not even had access to psychedelic mushrooms? Their brains were increasing as well. And this trend clearly goes back millions of years to the origin of our genus. The evolution of our brain is very unique, but it wasn't necessarily rapid. We were talking about 3 million years. There are plenty of other models that seek to explain why our brains got larger. And many models have hypothesized that the advent of cooking and meat consumption led to the surplus calories required to evolve a large brain. Other popular ideas surround social complexity and tool use. Ancient humans had an incredible amount of skills to master, places to remember, and relationships to keep intact. Such a demanding lifestyle may have led to the growth of the brain. In fact, both Neandertos and early modern humans had significantly larger brains than we have today. Over the past 20,000 years, we have lost about a tennis ball worth of brain volume. Our modern brains might be more efficient than the brains of the past, making a smaller brain better overall, but the point is, ancient humans had incredibly complex lives and needed bigger brains. But perhaps the biggest problem with McKenna's hypothesis is also the most fundamental. Evolution only works on traits that are heritable. That is, traits passed down through genes. Eating a mushroom might temporarily shift your perception, boost your mood, or even make you more socially outgoing. But it doesn't rewrite your DNA. Those effects vanish in hours. They're not passed on to your children. Now, to be fair, McKenna did try to address this. He speculated that psilocybin increased sexual arousal and socialability, which could theoretically lead to more mating and more offspring. In this hypothetical scenario, the individuals who consumed mushrooms would have reproduced more often, gradually tipping the genetic scales in their favor. But here's the issue. There's no evidence this ever happened. We have no data suggesting that regular mushroom users in ancient populations had more children or that those children inherited any cognitive benefits from their parents' trips. Even if psilocybin boosted libido in the moment, those behavioral effects are temporary, not biological upgrades that natural selection can act on. This line of thinking also brushes against Lamarian logic. The outdated idea that traits acquired during life can be passed down genetically, like suggesting if I lift weights and get strong, my kids will be born buff. It sounds intuitive, but evolution just doesn't work that way. So, even if mushrooms did make some people more social, more aroused, and more mystical, it's all moot unless those effects somehow translated to genetic advantages. And so far, there's no evidence they did. Well, the hypothesis is cool and all, but what do actual scientists think? As learned people of science, we should look at things skeptically and not take things at face value just because they tell a good story. Anthropologists, evolutionary biologists, and cognitive science have looked at the stone ape theory with deep skepticism. The problem isn't that psychedelics have no effects. They clearly do. The problem is connecting those effects to large-scale evolutionary changes in brain structure and behavior over millions of years. There is currently no fossil, genetic, or archaeological evidence supporting the idea that psilocybin had a direct impact on human evolution. No evidence of widespread psychedelic use that predates symbolic culture. And no way to test whether tripping apes were gained selective advantages that would have translated into reproductive success. Cognitive neuroscientist Dr. Andrew Gallamore has noted that while psychedelics may influence thought and perception, there's no scientific mechanism linking these experiences to the slow generational grind of evolution. Other experts like paleo anthropologist John Hawk have called the theory speculative at best. While open to interdisciplinary exploration, Hawk and others emphasize that our understanding of human evolution needs to be built on observable evidence, fossils, behavior, genetics, not on some what-if scenario. And there's another issue, dose and frequency. For a substance to influence evolution, it would need to be consumed regularly by a large portion of population over generations and lead to actual survival and reproductive advantages. That's a big ask for a mushroom that grows seasonally in specific conditions. Anthropologist Alisa Gua Dos considers the idea too simplistic, potentially a reduction of a complex evolutionary process into a single aha or oh well moment. She's also troubled by there being little evidence of such a pivotal moment or of drug use at all so early in the archaeological record. My initial thoughts about the stoned ape hypothesis was, well, that's a cool idea, but how would we ever even prove it? Hypothetically, we could find dental calculus from this early on showing mushroom consumption or mushroom residue on tools. Even if this evidence did exist, it would still be quite a stretch to correlate it to the gradual growth of our brains on multiple continents over 2 million years. discussion of the stoned ape hypothesis is certainly interesting and makes some good podcast clips, but scientifically it just doesn't stack up. What I find far more interesting is the influence psychedelic substances had on more recent stone age cultures. We know for a fact that cultures around the world use various psychoactive substances for thousands of years. But just how far does this go back? Around 40,000 years ago, human cultures in Africa, Europe, and Asia began to make complex art. Much of these paintings consisted of dreamlike depictions of animals and symbols. Though we don't have any tangible evidence that these paintings may have been influenced by psychedelics, the possibility is there. Cave paintings made 7,000 years ago in Algeria have a much clearer connection to these substances. The so-called mushroom shaman depicts a human figure with mushroom-like shape surrounding their head and limbs. Some interpret it as a spiritual figure engaged in a hallucinogenic ritual. Other paintings depict people hunting, dancing, and doing something else. Oh my god. Mushrooms may have very well had a large influence on the culture of this region. But shamanism was a much wider phenomenon than just North Africa. In fact, it was once the dominant religious practice throughout most of the world. This is very important for our understanding because the connection between shamanism and psychedelics is strong across many ancient traditions. Shamans often act as intermediaries between the physical and spiritual world. Using translike states to access knowledge, perform healing, or guide communities. Psychedelics weren't just recreational. They were sacred tools. In many cases, these substances were used in rights of passage, seasonal ceremonies, or healing rituals that reinforced group bonds and transmitted cultural knowledge. There is no doubt that psychedelics can be used to benefit cultures and societies, though I don't want to romanticize them. Psychedelic substances can vary widely in their effects and can be dangerous. Substances like dura used by the Aztecs are known to be extremely unpredictable. The substance itself is highly poisonous and there are truly terrifying horror stories about bad trips. Overall, psychedelics might have not had a large effect on our early evolution, but they could have helped shaped human culture or contributed to visionary experiences that informed early spiritual systems. Amanda Fielding of the psychedelic think tank Beckley Foundation says that the stoned ape theory is at very least a valid reminder that humans have always been drawn to mindaltering substances. The imagery that comes with the psychedelic experience is a theme that runs through ancient art. So, I'm sure that the psychedelic experience and other techniques like dancing and music were used by early ancestors to enhance consciousness which then facilitated spirituality, art, and medicine. So, did psychedelics make us human? Probably not. There's no evidence for that. But did they shape the way that early humans thought, felt, and connected with one another? Very possibly. What McKenna got right is that consciousness is strange, and evolution isn't just about brute survival. It's also about creativity, community, and meaning. And in that sense, psychedelics may have played a subtle but significant role in how human culture unfolded. They could have helped inspire early mythology, facilitated emotional healing, or offered insights into the self and the cosmos. Psychedelics in that context may have acted as mirrors or magnifiers of the human spirit. Still, it's important to separate poetry from science. The stone ape theory is a story, and a good one at that. But not every good story is true. The real story of human evolution is complex, multi-layered, and filled with mystery. It involves genetics, environment, diet, social behavior, tool use, and language. And yes, sometimes a little bit of shamanism. But if we're going to tell the story right, we need to anchor it in what we can test, trace, and understand. If you have watched my prior videos debunking wild evolution theories, you might see a common theme. Sensational ideas that tell a good story prosper in the public's imagination. Though the more sensational idea, the more critical we should be of it. Terrence McKenna seemed like a pretty cool dude, and I think his hypothesis has done a great job inspiring discourse about our evolution. It is always good to have a fresh take from a creative mind, so for that I applaud him. Though psychedelic mushrooms may have not been a driving force in our evolution, I bet some hominins were having a crazy time back in the day. Now, that is a topic for a good movie. Maybe a comedy movie, I'm not sure. Thanks for watching. If you made it this far, you're officially a part of the tribe. So, subscribe, comment what you want to see next, and hit that like button as hard as a shroodmed up homeerectus throwing a handax at a hippopotamus. And if you want more, YouTube thinks you'll watch this video.
